hook.rwenLine: A body scan is not a meditation. It's a survey. You move attention from your feet to your head, three seconds at a time, and notice what's there. That's all. No emptying, no clearing — just an honest look at the territory you live in.
hook.culturalNote: The body scan is the load-bearing technique of trauma-informed somatic work. Bessel van der Kolk's research on the body keeping the score points to a simple fact: nervous-system literacy starts with knowing what your nervous system feels like.

chunks.native: A slow, deliberate sweep of attention from feet to head, noticing what is present
chunks.literal: Jon Kabat-Zinn (technique); Bessel van der Kolk (trauma context); secular framing

chunks.native: Bringing attention to a body part without trying to change it
chunks.literal: ACT acceptance — turning toward rather than away from sensation

chunks.native: The fact that your body's state changes constantly — tight, loose, warm, cold, heavy, light
chunks.literal: Stoic 'all things flow' applied to bodily sensation; impermanence as observation, not philosophy
chunks.phonetic: What's there now wasn't there an hour ago.

pattern.name: Survey the territory before you try to change it.
pattern.explanation: Before you can regulate your nervous system you have to know what its current state actually is. The body scan is a five-minute survey: feet, calves, knees, thighs, hips, belly, lower back, upper back, chest, shoulders, arms, hands, neck, jaw, face, scalp. Three seconds each. No agenda except noticing. The state changes during the scan — that's the whole experience. You don't have to fix anything.

examples.native: Cold? Warm? Pressing down evenly? Heels heavier than toes?
Up around your ears? Falling forward? One higher than the other?
Clenched? Lips pressed together? Teeth touching or apart?

exercises.instruction: Match body part to what you'd notice

pairs.right: Pressure on the floor, warmth, weight

exercises.instruction: What's the goal of a body scan?
exercises.question: You're scanning and your shoulders are tight. What do you do?

options.text: Notice that they're tight. Move on. Maybe come back later.
Stop the scan because something's wrong

exercises.explanation: The scan is about noticing, not fixing. Naming 'shoulders tight' often releases some of the tension on its own — and even if it doesn't, you've got useful data.

exercises.instruction: Translate from instruction to body data
correct: My jaw is loose, mouth slightly open.

exercises.sentence: A body scan is a _____ , not a meditation.

exercises.translation: You can't regulate what you haven't located.

exercises.instruction: How long is enough for one scan?
exercises.question: You've got two minutes between meetings. Worth doing a scan?

options.text: Yes. Two minutes is enough. Three seconds per body region, top to bottom.
Only if you can also light a candle

exercises.explanation: Body scans scale. 90 seconds, three minutes, five minutes — any deliberate sweep is more interoception practice than no sweep.

rwenDialogue.intro: Let me walk you through a fast one. Two minutes. Feet to head. Just notice.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Start with your feet. Three seconds. What's there? Cold? Warm? Pressure on the heels or the toes?

userChoices.target: Feet are cold. More weight on my left heel.
userChoices.native: Specific body data — exactly the move.
userChoices.feedback: Yes. Two specifics in five seconds. Move on.
Honest. Wiggle them once. Try again in three seconds.
Fair question. We're doing this so you can tell the difference between 'I'm fine' and what your body is actually saying. Come back to the feet.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Good. Move up. Calves. Knees. Hips. Three seconds each. The whole scan takes two minutes. The data you collect — that's the win.

prompts.prompt: What's the goal of the scan?
How long for one body region?

mission.title: Today's mission
mission.task: Once today — ideally before bed or before a meeting — do a two-minute body scan. Feet to head. Three seconds each. No agenda. Just notice.
mission.rwenSignoff: You can't regulate what you haven't located. Tomorrow we work on what to do with what you found.

phase8.scenario: User has just completed their first solo body scan. Rwen debriefs — what did they notice? Where was tension? Where was ease?
phase8.rwenRole: A calm, curious companion who treats the user's findings as interesting data, not pathology.
phase8.successCriteria: User has named at least four specific body regions and what was there. Bonus if they noticed something change during the scan.
